Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: fixed verbal clause; noun igi “eye/front” + participial/adverbial bar-ra “outside” + finite speech verb du₁₁ “to say”
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: to speak openly and plainly; to make something clear before others
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: eme-zu šu nu-ub-bal-e; inim-zu ge₁₇-e du₁₁-ga
Usage Contexts: council speech, dispute settlement, teaching, formal clarification
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Built from well-attested Sumerian imagery of the eye/front as public visibility and the verb du₁₁ for speech or legal declaration.
First Example Sentence: ensi₂-e igi bar-ra du₁₁, lú-še₃ inim ge₁₇-e ĝen-na.
Third Example Sentence: di ku₅-ru-na, inim-bi igi bar-ra du₁₁-ga-am₃.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: object–verb idiom; noun ĝeštug₂ “ear/wisdom” + adjective ḫulu “bad” + transitive verb dab₅ “to seize”
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: to misunderstand badly; to take advice in the wrong way
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Uses the ear as a seat of hearing, obedience, and wisdom, a recurrent image in scribal and wisdom literature.
Second Example Sentence: ama-ni inim gal du₁₁-ga, dumu-ne ĝeštug₂ ḫulu dab₅.
Third Example Sentence: lú nu-zu-e ĝeštug₂ ḫulu dab₅, nam-lú-u₁₈-lu nu-mu-un-zu.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: finite verbal phrase; body-part noun šu “hand” + adjective/adverb si “straight/right” + ventive-prefix verbal form of sa₂ “to equal, set right”
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: to set matters right; to act properly and restore order
Usage Contexts: workplace, temple administration, household management, moral advice
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Draws on the Sumerian value of right ordering, fairness, and correct ritual or administrative action.
Second Example Sentence: dumu munus-e še gur₁₀-ra šu si im-mi-in-sa₂.
Third Example Sentence: lugal-e iri-na šu si im-mi-in-sa₂, kur-bi ba-an-da-sig₁₀.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: fixed nominal-verbal phrase; ka “mouth” + participial tar-ra “cut” + intransitive/locative verb gub “stand”
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: to be silenced; to stop speaking because of fear, shame, or authority
Usage Contexts: court, hierarchy, sarcasm, family disputes, political speech
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: The mouth marks speech and social standing; “cut mouth” evokes loss of voice in public or official settings.
First Example Sentence: sukkal-maḫ-e inim du₁₁-ga, lú še₃ ka tar-ra mu-un-gub.
Second Example Sentence: diĝir-ra inim gal-la, inim-zu ka tar-ra mu-un-gub.
Third Example Sentence: šeš-a-ne-ne ĝeštu₂-ga in-ne-ne, ka tar-ra mu-un-gub-bu-ne.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: locative verbal idiom; kur-ra “in/on the foreign land or mountain” + ĝiri₃ “foot” + gub “stand”
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: to face danger or uncertainty away from familiar protection
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: ki nu-zu-a gub; a₂ tuku-a bar-ra ĝen
Usage Contexts: travel, military, exile, trade, heroic narrative
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Kur represents foreign lands, mountains, and hostile territory in Sumerian cosmology and historical writing.
First Example Sentence: dam-gar₃-e kur-ra ĝiri₃ gub, nig₂-sa₁₀-bi mu-un-ĝar.
Second Example Sentence: erin₂-e kur-ra ĝiri₃ gub-ba, a₂ gal im-mi-in-ak.
Third Example Sentence: lú tur-ra kur-ra ĝiri₃ gub, ama-ni inim-zu mu-un-ĝal₂.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: negative existential idiom; ĝeš “wood/tool” + locative šu-a “in the hand” + negated existential nu-ĝal₂
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: to lack the means, tools, or authority to complete a task
Usage Contexts: work, administration, craft, agriculture, requests for resources
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Reflects craft and labor culture where the hand and tool together represent ability and agency.
Second Example Sentence: engar-ra ĝeš šu-a nu-ĝal₂, a-ša₃ nu-mu-un-sig₁₀.
Third Example Sentence: dub-sar-ra dub nu-ĝal₂, ĝeš šu-a nu-ĝal₂-gen₇ ba-an-du₁₁.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: passive/medio-passive clause; inim “word/matter” + genitive kalam-ma “of the land” + šu ba-ti “was received/taken in hand”
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: a public issue has been formally accepted for action or judgment
Usage Contexts: administration, legal proceedings, civic affairs, royal inscriptions
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Evokes temple-palace bureaucracy, where taking something into the hand marks responsibility and official handling.
First Example Sentence: inim kalam-ma šu ba-ti, di-ku₅-ne šag₄-bi im-mi-in-zu.
Second Example Sentence: ensi₂-ra inim kalam-ma šu ba-ti-a mu-un-na-an-du₁₁.
Third Example Sentence: dub-ba inim kalam-ma šu ba-ti, u₄-bi mu-na-an-ĝar.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: emotive existential clause; šag₄ “heart/interior” + adjective tur “small” + comitative/ventive existential verbal form
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: to feel anxious, timid, or inwardly diminished
Usage Contexts: emotion, family talk, prayer, confession, uncertainty
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: The heart/interior is a major Sumerian locus of feeling, intention, and courage.
First Example Sentence: u₄ diĝir gal-ra, šag₄ tur mu-un-da-an-ĝal₂.
Second Example Sentence: dumu-ni kur-ra ba-ĝen, ama-a šag₄ tur mu-un-da-an-ĝal₂.
Third Example Sentence: nig₂-gig-bi igi-na ba-an-tuku, šag₄ tur mu-un-da-an-ĝal₂.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: object–verb idiom; abstract noun nam-lú-u₁₈-lu “humanity/human condition” + šu la₂ “to bind/hold by hand”
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: to act humanely; to hold fast to proper social responsibility
Usage Contexts: ethics, leadership, community aid, teaching, lamentation
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Centers on nam-lú-u₁₈-lu, the Sumerian idea of human nature, social duty, and civilized conduct.
Second Example Sentence: geme₂ arad₂-ra ninda ba-an-šum₂, nam-lú-u₁₈-lu šu la₂-a-am₃.
Third Example Sentence: iri ḫul-a-ne, lú sag₉-ga nam-lú-u₁₈-lu šu la₂.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: finite verbal idiom; a₂ “arm/strength” + ventive finite form of šub “to fall/drop”
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: to lose strength, momentum, or authority; to be worn down
Usage Contexts: work fatigue, politics, military defeat, emotional discouragement
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: The arm signifies power and capability; its falling suggests defeat, exhaustion, or reduced agency.
First Example Sentence: u₄-da a₂ im-ma-an-šub, engar-ra e₂-a ba-an-ku₄.
Second Example Sentence: erin₂-ne kur-ra a₂ im-ma-an-šub, uru-še₃ ba-ĝen-ne.
Third Example Sentence: dub-sar tur-e dub gal-am₃ mu-un-sar, ĝešgigir-gen₇ a₂ im-ma-an-šub.
